destinations.name: Bijapur
destinations.description: **Bijapur**, now known officially as **Vijayapura** (Kannada: ವಿಜಯಪುರ), is the district headquarters of Bijapur District in a city in Northwestern Karnataka . It was once the capital of the Bijapur Sultanate, ruled by the Adil Shahi dynasty. Tombs, palaces, mosques, darghas and a portion of the fortified citadel still stand; this includes the Gol Gumbaz. The Gol Gumbaz houses the second largest dome in the world.

understand.brief: After the fall of the Bahamni Sultanate in 1490, Bujapur came under the control of Adil Shahi dynasty. From 1490 to 1686 Bijapur served as their capital. For almost two centuries, Bijapur was converted into a fortified citadel surrounded with towering walls complete with massive bastions and gateways. The Adil Shahi sultans came up with magnificent palaces, tombs and mosques, turning the city into a medieval wonder. The Gol Gambuz is the most well-known monument of Bijapur and houses the second-largest dome in the world. Bijapur has a share of medieval heritage and attracts heritage and history lovers. Sadly, the town lacks proper tourist infrastructure.

get_in.brief:
Bijapur is well connected to the rest of the country by rail and road. The airport is yet to start operations. 


 By air 1 **Jagatjyoti Shri Basaveshwar Airport**. The Bijapur airport is under construction and likely to open at the end of 2025.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 By train 2 **Bijapur railway station** . The railway station (Station Code : BJP) is linked with Mumbai , Bangalore , Pune , Hyderabad and other major cities. There are four trains per day from Badami to Bijapur, the journey takes two and a half hours.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 By bus 3 **KSRTC Bus Stand**. Bijapur is also well connected with other cities by bus. There are five buses from Badami to Bijapur per day. The journey lasts four hours. 530 km northwest of Bangalore and about 550 km from Mumbai. From Mumbai, there are direct bus services to Bijapur (but very few). The most convenient option is to take a bus to Belgaum (there are more than 25-30 buses). From Belgaum Central bus stand, there are regular express buses (every 15-20 minutes) to Bijapur. The journey takes 3 hours or so.  ( updated Sep 2025 )

get_around.brief:
Most of them can be covered on a combination of foot and auto rickshaw. Mahatma Gandhi Square (chowk) is the main place and it is situated in the heart of the city. It is the main market place for the locals and also for the people from towns nearby.


 Auto Rickshaw There are a lot of rickshaws in town. Their drivers know all the major attractions of the town. Since no rickshaw has a meter, you need to negotiate their prices and bargain very hard.


 Tonga (Horse carriage) A very quaint way to get around, especially for tourists, is by tonga or horse buggy as it's called. There is a horse buggy stand at MG Square. Alternatively, outside Gol Gumbaz, there are many horse buggies waiting for business. Negotiate a round figure for the whole day and the tonga will take you to all the important places. A small tip at the end (in the name of the horse - tell the driver to buy something special for the horse) will get you a friend for life. **Note:** These rides are not animal-friendly

itinerary.name: Gol Gumbaz
itinerary.description: It is the second-largest dome and largest unsupported dome in the world. It is the mausoleum of Mohammed Adil Shah and his family. It houses 5 cenotaphs (the original graves are located in the crypt below). Apart from Mohammed Adil Shah the cenotaphs belong to two of his wives, a mistress, a daughter and a grandson. It was built in 1659. At the top, the whispering gallery is a wonder where a tick of a watch can be heard from the opposite side of the dome over 200 feet away. It has a clock room for storing luggage.
itinerary.price: ₹25 for Indians, SAARC and BIMSTEC. ₹300 for other foreigners. (Childern below 15 free). Videography (Handycam, for personal use): ₹25. Photography (still): Free

itinerary.name: Gol Gumbaz Site Museum
itinerary.description: The Gol Gumbaz Site Museum is housed inside the Maqar Khana (drum house). It houses 8 galleries spread over two floors.

itinerary.name: Jumma Masjid
itinerary.description: A Mosque built by Ali Adil Shah I over 300 years ago for the entire city (jumma = public gathering). The Kuran inscriptions in gold is the main attraction. It can contain 2250 devotees. The intricately carved mihrab (the niche that points the direction to Mecca) is absolutely brilliant and is in stark contrast to the generally simple and bare architecture of the mosque itself (which is quite typical for the Deccan monuments)

itinerary.name: Taj Bawdi (Tasabavadi)
itinerary.description: A stepwell built by Ibrahim Adil Shah II in honour of his wife, Queen Taj Sultana, in the year 1620. The stepwell complex is approached through a large arched gateway. The complex also houses a hammam.

itinerary.name: Jod Gumbaz
itinerary.description: The tombs date back to the late Adil Shahi period and house the mortal remains of Khan Muhammad (octagonal tomb) and his spiritual advisor, Abdul Razzaq Qadiri (square tomb). Khan Muhammad was a commander of the Adil Shahi army. He betrayed them and favoured the Mughals. He was executed. The tomb was founded by Mughal emperor Aurangzeb. The tomb complex also houses another smaller tomb. The complex is an active dargah and is visited by a large number of devotees.

itinerary.name: Bara Kaman
itinerary.description: Ali Adil Shah II began building a beautiful structure in 1627 with 12 arches in 12 rows, 12 columns, and 12 storeys tall, so that its shadow would fall on Golgumbaz in the evening. He died before it got completed. Nowadays, the unfinished monument can be visited.

itinerary.name: Upali Buruj
itinerary.description: This is a large structure around 80 feet tall built in 1584. Approachable by a long flight of stairs. Has 2 cannons at the top. You can get a 360-degree view of Bijapur town from the top.

itinerary.name: Malik E Maidan
itinerary.description: Translated as the _king of the field,_ this is a huge cannon made of "Panchaloha" or five metals. It weighs 55 tons. This cannon helped Bijapur to defeat mighty the Vijayanagar empire in the battle of Rakkasatangadi (Talikot) in 1565. Today, it stands atop a bastion on the western wall of Bijapur Fort. It is located just north of the Mecca gate.

itinerary.name: Ibrahim Rauza mausoleum
itinerary.description: Ibrahim Rauza mausoleum is located outside the citadel walls but within the city limits. It houses the mausoleum of Ibrahim Adil Shah II. The complex is accessible via a gateway in the north. It houses the mausoleum on the east and a mosque on the west. Both the mosque and the mausoleum stand on an elevated platform with a water cistern in the middle. The mausoleum houses several graves. It was constructed in 1626.
itinerary.price: Entrance ₹5 for Indians and ₹300 for foreigners

itinerary.name: Asar Mahal
itinerary.description: This building was constructed in 1646 by Sultan Mohammed. It served as a justice hall. On the eastern side lies a large water body. There are several ruined structures in the complex. The Asar Mahal functions as a mosque.

itinerary.name: Anand Mahal
itinerary.description: This was private residence of the king with the queen, used for relaxing and pleasure, hence the name Anand Mahal. Today it houses several state government offices.

itinerary.name: Gagan Mahal
itinerary.description: Built by Ali Adil Shah I, the fifth ruler of Adil Shahi dynasty, in 1561. Gagan Mahal has two floors. The ground floor of Gagan Mahal was the place for the audience hall or durbar hall. The second floor of Gagan Mahal was used as a residence for the royal family. The front facade consists of a wide arch flanked by smaller arches. Today, the Gagan Mahal is beautifully restored with a large open space, which serves as a socializing place for locals and tourists.

itinerary.name: Sat Mahal
itinerary.description: A seven-storeyed palace. It is built by Ibrahim Adil Shah II around 1583 as a corner structure within the citadel, which also served as a watchtower. It is in ruins and only 5 of the seven stories survive.

itinerary.name: Jal Mahal
itinerary.description: The slender domed structure stands in the middle of a small water tank, which has dried up

itinerary.name: Andu Masjid
itinerary.description: The mosque was built in 1608/09 CE by I'tibar Khan, a noble in the court of Ibrahim Adil Shah II. It is a two-story building following the Indo-Islamic style of architecture. The mosque occupies the first floor and part of the ground floor. The remaining part of the first floor probably served as a _sarai._ The first floor contains an open terrace surrounded by low parapet. The structure is topped with a central ribbed dome and is flanked by four minarates.

itinerary.name: Mehtar Mahal
itinerary.description: Mehtar Mahal is a three-storied palace located on the southern side of the Bijapur citadel. It was constructed in the early 17th century under the rule of Ibrahim Adil Shah II. It follows the Indo-Saracenic style of architecture and is complete with slender minarates and ornate oriel windows. The palace is accessible through a giant, decorative teakwood door. The complex also houses an active mosque dating back to the sultanate period.

itinerary.name: Fateh Darwaza
itinerary.description: Southern gateway. Complete with bastions and guardhouses. Today traffic passes through this gateway.

itinerary.name: Mecca Gate
itinerary.description: Western Gateway. Totally encroached and not accessible.

itinerary.name: Shahapur Gate
itinerary.description: North-western Gate

itinerary.name: Alapur Gate
itinerary.description: Located near Gol Gumbaz next to the railway line. The gate is encroached.

itinerary.name: Bahamani Gate
itinerary.description: Northern Gate

itinerary.name: Jahan Begum's Tomb
itinerary.description: Jahan Begum was the wife of Muhammad Adil Shah. The tomb is unfinished and lies in ruins. The tomb, made of black, polished granite, is enclosed inside an arched pavilion, whose roof has long since collapsed. The four ruined minars stand at the corner of the enclosure. The entire area is a walled complex with a mosque on the southern side.

itinerary.name: Ain-ul-Mulk's Tomb
itinerary.description: The tomb contains the mortal remains of Ain-ul-Mulk, a nobleman in the court of Ibrahim I. He rebelled against the sultan and was killed in 1556. The structure stands on a square base and is topped with a hemispherical dome. Each of the four corners is provided with a domed pavilion. The outer walls are decorated with intricate stucco work. The facade has two tiers of three recessed arches on all its sides. The huge complex also houses a mosque and several other ruined structures.

itinerary.name: Jahan Begum Palace
itinerary.description: The palace consists of a single-storied structure. It is in ruins. A few arches and jalis are all that remains of the grand palace.

itinerary.name: Shivgiri
itinerary.description: This is a new structure. At 80 ft (24 m) tall, it is the largest statue of Lord Shiva in the world.

itinerary.name: Sangeeth Nari Mahal
itinerary.description: It is a mystery how this great theater of music remained undiscovered until 1990, but when it was found the scientists were amazed about the acoustics used. In the memory of this find, Bijapur celebrates "Navarasapur Utsav" every 5 years where renowned musicians from across India come to perform

itinerary.name: Saat Kabar
itinerary.description: Saat Kabar (Literally meaning 60 graves) is believed to house the 63 graves of the wives of Afzal Khan, a general under Adil Shah II. According to legend, Khan had his wives murdered out of jealousy to prevent them from remarrying after he died in battle with Shivaji. The graves are arranged in a rectangular formation, with a mosque on the western side. To the west of the grave complex lies a small step well.

see.brief:
A standard tour of Bijapur consists of visiting the 8 popular places. They include Gol Gumbaz, Jami Masjid, Taj Bawdi, Jod Gumbaz, Bara Kaman, Upali Bruz, Malik e Madan and Ibrahim Roza. Autos and Tongas offer this package tour. Both auto and tonga drivers carry a folded leaflet describing the 8 major sites of Bijapur. These leaflet can also be purchased for ₹10 at most of the sites. 


 1 **Gol Gumbaz** ( Gol Gummat ), Entrance on Station Road . 06:00 - 18:00 . It is the second-largest dome and largest unsupported dome in the world. It is the mausoleum of Mohammed Adil Shah and his family. It houses 5 cenotaphs (the original graves are located in the crypt below). Apart from Mohammed Adil Shah the cenotaphs belong to two of his wives, a mistress, a daughter and a grandson. It was built in 1659. At the top, the whispering gallery is a wonder where a tick of a watch can be heard from the opposite side of the dome over 200 feet away. It has a clock room for storing luggage. ₹25 for Indians, SAARC and BIMSTEC. ₹300 for other foreigners. (Childern below 15 free). Videography (Handycam, for personal use): ₹25. Photography (still): Free .   
 2 **Gol Gumbaz Mosque** , West side of Gol Gumbaz complex . The mosque is located on the western end of the Gol Gumbaz complex. The mosque is best viewed from the top of Gol Gumbaz. It is not accessible to visitors.   
 3 **Gol Gumbaz Site Museum**. The Gol Gumbaz Site Museum is housed inside the Maqar Khana (drum house). It houses 8 galleries spread over two floors.  ( updated Oct 2025 ) 
 4 **Jumma Masjid** ( Jamiya Masjid ). A Mosque built by Ali Adil Shah I over 300 years ago for the entire city (jumma = public gathering). The Kuran inscriptions in gold is the main attraction. It can contain 2250 devotees. The intricately carved mihrab (the niche that points the direction to Mecca) is absolutely brilliant and is in stark contrast to the generally simple and bare architecture of the mosque itself (which is quite typical for the Deccan monuments) Free entrance .   
 5 **Taj Bawdi (Tasabavadi)** . A stepwell built by Ibrahim Adil Shah II in honour of his wife, Queen Taj Sultana, in the year 1620. The stepwell complex is approached through a large arched gateway. The complex also houses a hammam.   
 6 **Jod Gumbaz** ( Twin Tombs ), East of Taj Bawadi . The tombs date back to the late Adil Shahi period and house the mortal remains of Khan Muhammad (octagonal tomb) and his spiritual advisor, Abdul Razzaq Qadiri (square tomb). Khan Muhammad was a commander of the Adil Shahi army. He betrayed them and favoured the Mughals. He was executed. The tomb was founded by Mughal emperor Aurangzeb. The tomb complex also houses another smaller tomb. The complex is an active dargah and is visited by a large number of devotees.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 7 **Bara Kaman** ( Near Station Road ). Ali Adil Shah II began building a beautiful structure in 1627 with 12 arches in 12 rows, 12 columns, and 12 storeys tall, so that its shadow would fall on Golgumbaz in the evening. He died before it got completed. Nowadays, the unfinished monument can be visited.   
 8 **Upali Buruj** ( Upli Burz ) ( Near Malik E Maidan ). This is a large structure around 80 feet tall built in 1584. Approachable by a long flight of stairs. Has 2 cannons at the top. You can get a 360-degree view of Bijapur town from the top.   
 9 **Malik E Maidan** ( Near Mahatma Gandhi Road ). Translated as the _king of the field,_ this is a huge cannon made of "Panchaloha" or five metals. It weighs 55 tons. This cannon helped Bijapur to defeat mighty the Vijayanagar empire in the battle of Rakkasatangadi (Talikot) in 1565. Today, it stands atop a bastion on the western wall of Bijapur Fort. It is located just north of the Mecca gate.   
 10 **Ibrahim Rauza mausoleum** . 06:00 - 18:00 . Ibrahim Rauza mausoleum is located outside the citadel walls but within the city limits. It houses the mausoleum of Ibrahim Adil Shah II. The complex is accessible via a gateway in the north. It houses the mausoleum on the east and a mosque on the west. Both the mosque and the mausoleum stand on an elevated platform with a water cistern in the middle. The mausoleum houses several graves. It was constructed in 1626. Entrance ₹5 for Indians and ₹300 for foreigners .   
 lesser known sites Apart from the 8 major sites, Bijapur has several other historical sites. This can also be visited by paying some extra money to the auto. However, there are no fixed rates. 


 11 **Asar Mahal** ( Entrance on Station Road ). This building was constructed in 1646 by Sultan Mohammed. It served as a justice hall. On the eastern side lies a large water body. There are several ruined structures in the complex. The Asar Mahal functions as a mosque.   
 12 **Anand Mahal** ( Palace of happiness ). This was private residence of the king with the queen, used for relaxing and pleasure, hence the name Anand Mahal. Today it houses several state government offices.   
 13 **Gagan Mahal** ( Palace of the skies ). Built by Ali Adil Shah I, the fifth ruler of Adil Shahi dynasty, in 1561. Gagan Mahal has two floors. The ground floor of Gagan Mahal was the place for the audience hall or durbar hall. The second floor of Gagan Mahal was used as a residence for the royal family. The front facade consists of a wide arch flanked by smaller arches. Today, the Gagan Mahal is beautifully restored with a large open space, which serves as a socializing place for locals and tourists.   
 14 **Sat Mahal** ( Sat Manjil ). A seven-storeyed palace. It is built by Ibrahim Adil Shah II around 1583 as a corner structure within the citadel, which also served as a watchtower. It is in ruins and only 5 of the seven stories survive.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 15 **Jal Mahal** ( Water Palace ) ( Opposite Sat Mahal ). The slender domed structure stands in the middle of a small water tank, which has dried up  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 16 **Andu Masjid** ( Anda Masjid ). The mosque was built in 1608/09 CE by I'tibar Khan, a noble in the court of Ibrahim Adil Shah II. It is a two-story building following the Indo-Islamic style of architecture. The mosque occupies the first floor and part of the ground floor. The remaining part of the first floor probably served as a _sarai._ The first floor contains an open terrace surrounded by low parapet. The structure is topped with a central ribbed dome and is flanked by four minarates.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 17 **Mehtar Mahal** . Mehtar Mahal is a three-storied palace located on the southern side of the Bijapur citadel. It was constructed in the early 17th century under the rule of Ibrahim Adil Shah II. It follows the Indo-Saracenic style of architecture and is complete with slender minarates and ornate oriel windows. The palace is accessible through a giant, decorative teakwood door. The complex also houses an active mosque dating back to the sultanate period.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 Gates of the citadel The citadel of Bijapur has 5 gates 


 18 **Fateh Darwaza** ( Mongoli Base ). Southern gateway. Complete with bastions and guardhouses. Today traffic passes through this gateway.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 19 **Mecca Gate**. Western Gateway. Totally encroached and not accessible.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 20 **Shahapur Gate**. North-western Gate  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 21 **Alapur Gate** ( Eastern Gate ). Located near Gol Gumbaz next to the railway line. The gate is encroached.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 22 **Bahamani Gate**. Northern Gate  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 Outside Bijapur The ruins of Bijapur extend beyond the fortified citadel. There are several monuments located on the east and west of the citadel.


 East of Citadel 23 **Jahan Begum's Tomb** . Jahan Begum was the wife of Muhammad Adil Shah. The tomb is unfinished and lies in ruins. The tomb, made of black, polished granite, is enclosed inside an arched pavilion, whose roof has long since collapsed. The four ruined minars stand at the corner of the enclosure. The entire area is a walled complex with a mosque on the southern side.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 24 **Ain-ul-Mulk's Tomb** ( Chota Gol Gumbaj ) ( Located outsited the citadel (Eastern side) ). The tomb contains the mortal remains of Ain-ul-Mulk, a nobleman in the court of Ibrahim I. He rebelled against the sultan and was killed in 1556. The structure stands on a square base and is topped with a hemispherical dome. Each of the four corners is provided with a domed pavilion. The outer walls are decorated with intricate stucco work. The facade has two tiers of three recessed arches on all its sides. The huge complex also houses a mosque and several other ruined structures.  ( updated Aug 2025 ) 
 25 **Jahan Begum Palace**, Located south-west of the Jahan Begum Tomb complex . The palace consists of a single-storied structure. It is in ruins. A few arches and jalis are all that remains of the grand palace.  ( updated Sep 2025 ) 
 26 **Shivgiri** ( 80 ft tall statue of Shiva ). This is a new structure. At 80 ft (24 m) tall, it is the largest statue of Lord Shiva in the world. Entrance ₹3 .   
 West of Citadel 27 **Sangeeth Nari Mahal** ( Navarasapura ). It is a mystery how this great theater of music remained undiscovered until 1990, but when it was found the scientists were amazed about the acoustics used. In the memory of this find, Bijapur celebrates "Navarasapur Utsav" every 5 years where renowned musicians from across India come to perform   
 28 **Saat Kabar**. Saat Kabar (Literally meaning 60 graves) is believed to house the 63 graves of the wives of Afzal Khan, a general under Adil Shah II. According to legend, Khan had his wives murdered out of jealousy to prevent them from remarrying after he died in battle with Shivaji. The graves are arranged in a rectangular formation, with a mosque on the western side. To the west of the grave complex lies a small step well.  ( updated Sep 2025 )

activities.name: Bijapur Market
activities.description: You can wander around this crowded and noisy market and see the vegetables and spices which are sold there.

do.brief: You can wander around this crowded and noisy market and see the vegetables and spices which are sold there.

items.name: Kamat restaurant
items.description: Excellent veg Indian food.

items.name: Mysore Restaurant
items.description: A nice veg restaurant which is usually crowded for lunch. At the last floor, it offer a nice view of the market.

items.name: Local drinks
items.description:
The drinking places of Bijapur are a little squalid. If you would like to have a drink it is more pleasant to do that during day time. The local bars are patronised by the rural population who like to gulp the entire contents of their quarter sized whisky bottle in go. It is an experience for those who like to observe local customs.

Most of the decent hotels on Station Road have a more 'civilised' bar with air conditioning. The range of alcohol choices may be limited i.e. mostly Indian made brands.

items.name: Hotel Golden Heights
items.description: A decent hotel, more suited for the business traveller. The restaurant on the ground floor is pure vegetarian while the bar-restaurant on the top floor serves non-vegetarian. They have an excellent biriyani.

items.name: Hotel Megharaj Lodge
items.description: Simple and well located hotel in the center of the town. A/C and non A/C rooms are available.

destinations.name: Chikmagalur
destinations.description: **Chikmagalur**, officially **Chikkamagaluru** ( Kannada : ಚಿಕ್ಕಮಗಳೂರು), is a city in Karnataka , known for its coffee plantations and Bababudan Giri ranges.

understand.brief:
Chikmagalur city (Chikkamagaluru district) is at the foot of highest mountain range in Karnataka. The life-supporting rivers of Karnataka and other states - Tunga, Bhadra, Hemavati, Vedavati, Yagachi originate in the Western Ghat hills of the district.

The district is a great attraction to all kinds of people - trekkers, pilgrims, travellers and tourists. This area is famous for the coffee estates in the vicinity and also known as the Coffee Belt of the South.

get_in.brief:
By plane Mangalore airport (** IXE **  IATA ), 170 km Bangalore airport (** BLR **  IATA ), 250 km.


 By train Passenger trains are available from Shimoga and Kadur at a distance of 40 km.

Arrivals and departures from Chikkamagaluru Railway Station


 Train Number 56271: Shivamogga-Chikkamagaluru Passenger-Departs Shivamogga at 18:15 and arrives Chikkamagaluru at 21:30 [via; Kadur Jn] 
 Train Number 56272: Chikkamagaluru-Shivamogga Passenger-Departs Chikkamagaluru at 06:30 and arrives Shivamogga at 09:30 [via; Kadur Jn] 
 Train Number 56277: Chikkamagaluru-Yeshvantpur Jn Fast Passenger-Departs Chikkamagaluru at 07:30 and arrives Yeshvantpur Jn at 14:15 [via; Kadur Jn] 
 Train Number 56278: Yeshvantpur Jn-Chikkamagaluru Fast Passenger-Departs Yeshvantpur Jn at 15:15 and arrives Chikkamagaluru at 22:20 [via; Kadur Jn] 
 1 **Chikmagalur railway station** .  ( updated Apr 2020 ) 
 By bus Chikmagalur is located on the highway from Hassan towards Shringeri and has regular Luxury as well as ordinary buses from Bangalore . Regular ordinary buses connect Chikmagalur to Mysore , Hassan with a single services to Kozhikode . Buses from interior Karnataka towards Mangalore too pass via Chikmagalur and hence, ordinary buses connect Chikmaglur regularly to Dharmastala , Mangalore , Shimoga , Davangere and Sringeri.


 By car You can drive to Chikmagalur comfortably by road from Hassan 60 km, which is again 185 km from Bangalore .


 By taxi/car rental You can hire a taxi or rent a car from Bangalore to visit Chikmagalur. Out station car rental companies charge for a minimum of 250 km/day @ a fare/km rate + driver charges (BA/TA: Boarding Allowance Travel Allowance). The fares depend upon the type of car you are looking for. It is advisable to rent cars from reliable and established car rental operators.

get_around.brief:
By autorickshaw Drivers don't go by meters. They fix a charge for the place you want to go; bargaining is up to you. The more your attire and luggage announce that you are a tourist the greater the chance of drivers ripping you off.


 By taxi Tourist cabs are the best choice in case you want to tour the city all day or visit nearby places like Kemmannagundi . Costs are about ₹10 per kilometre.

There is only one tea shop at B.B.Hills, so you have to beg for a plate of hot porottas. But you can skip the eating as the scenes are terrific.
